content:
# Exodus Chapter 37 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 108–109; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 80–81 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_037_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_037_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_037_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_037_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Ark of the Covenant (vv. 1–9)

**1** Bezalel made the ark out of acacia wood — three and three-quarter feet long, two and a quarter feet wide, and two and a quarter feet tall. **2** He overlaid it with pure gold inside and out, and fashioned a border of gold molding all around it. **3** He cast four gold rings for its four feet — two rings on one side and two rings on the other. **4** He made carrying poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with gold, **5** then slid the poles through the rings on either side of the ark so it could be carried.

**6** He made the mercy seat of pure gold — three and three-quarter feet long and two and a quarter feet wide. **7** He made two cherubim of hammered gold, shaping them from a single piece at each end of the mercy seat. **8** One cherub stood at one end and the other at the opposite end — he made the cherubim as a single piece with the mercy seat itself, one at each end. **9** The cherubim had their wings spread upward, overshadowing the mercy seat with them, and they faced each other, with their faces turned down toward the mercy seat.

---

### The Table for the Bread of the Presence (vv. 10–16)

**10** He made the table of acacia wood — three feet long, eighteen inches wide, and two and a quarter feet tall. **11** He overlaid it with pure gold and fashioned a border of gold molding all around it. **12** He made a rim around it about three inches wide, with a border of gold molding around the rim. **13** He cast four gold rings for it and attached the rings to the four corners at its four legs. **14** The rings were placed close to the rim to hold the poles for carrying the table. **15** He made the carrying poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with gold. **16** He also made the vessels that belonged on the table — its plates, its ladles, its bowls, and its pitchers for pouring out drink offerings — all of pure gold.

---

### The Lampstand (vv. 17–24)

**17** He made the lampstand of pure gold. He hammered the lampstand out of a single piece — its base and its shaft, its cup-shaped flowers, its buds, and its blossoms all formed one piece with it. **18** Six branches extended from its sides — three branches from one side and three from the other. **19** Three cups shaped like almond blossoms, each with a bud and petals, were on one branch, and three cups shaped like almond blossoms, each with a bud and petals, were on the next branch — and so on for all six branches extending from the lampstand. **20** On the central shaft of the lampstand itself there were four cups shaped like almond blossoms, complete with buds and petals. **21** There was a bud beneath each pair of branches where they extended from the shaft — one bud under the first pair, one under the second pair, and one under the third pair, corresponding to the six branches. **22** The buds and branches were all of one piece with the lampstand, the whole of it a single piece of hammered pure gold. **23** He made its seven lamps, along with its wick trimmers and its trays, all of pure gold. **24** He used a talent of pure gold for the lampstand and all its accessories.

---

### The Altar of Incense (vv. 25–28)

**25** He made the incense altar out of acacia wood — eighteen inches long, eighteen inches wide (a perfect square), and three feet tall. Its horns were of one piece with it. **26** He overlaid its top, all its sides, and its horns with pure gold, and fashioned a border of gold molding all around it. **27** He made two gold rings for it below the molding on two of its opposite sides, to hold the poles for carrying it. **28** He made the carrying poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with gold.

---

### The Sacred Anointing Oil and the Incense (v. 29)

**29** He also prepared the sacred anointing oil and the pure, fragrant incense — the work of a master perfumer.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verse 1 — "Bezalel made the ark"
The Hebrew וַיַּעַשׂ בְּצַלְאֵל (vayyaas Betsalel, "and Bezalel made") names the craftsman explicitly at the beginning of this chapter, though in the parallel construction of the tabernacle curtains (ch. 36), the subject was more diffuse. The ark — אֲרוֹן (aron) — is the first furnishing described, reflecting its supreme importance as the locus of YHWH's presence. The Leningrad Codex text is clear and well-preserved here. The LXX in Vaticanus presents this material in a significantly different arrangement (see Vaticanus Divergences below), but the content itself — the dimensions, materials, and construction details — is largely consistent between the two traditions. The Hebrew measurements are two and a half cubits by one and a half cubits by one and a half cubits. A cubit (אַמָּה, ammah) is approximately eighteen inches (45 cm), yielding the conversions given.

### Verses 6–9 — The mercy seat and cherubim
The כַּפֹּרֶת (kapporet, "mercy seat" or "atonement cover") is arguably the single most theologically laden object in the entire tabernacle. The root כ-פ-ר (k-p-r) means "to cover, to atone, to ransom" — the kapporet is the place where atonement happens, where the blood of the Day of Atonement sacrifice was sprinkled (Lev 16:14–15). The LXX renders ἱλαστήριον (hilasterion, "place of propitiation"), a word Paul will later apply to Christ (Rom 3:25). The cherubim — כְּרוּבִים (keruvim) — were hammered מִקְשָׁה (miqshah, "of hammered work"), shaped from the same piece of gold as the mercy seat itself. This is not merely an aesthetic choice but a theological statement: the cherubim who overshadow the place of atonement are inseparable from it. They face each other, with their gazes directed downward toward the kapporet — toward the very place where, between them, YHWH would speak to Moses (25:22). The dynamic rendering "mercy seat" follows the tradition established by Tyndale and preserved through the KJV, capturing the functional meaning: this is where mercy is enacted.

### Verse 16 — The table vessels
The four types of vessels — קְעָרֹת (qe'arot, "plates/dishes"), כַּפֹּת (kappot, "ladles/spoons"), קְשׂוֹת (qesot, "bowls/jars"), and מְנַקִּיֹּת (menaqqiyyot, "pitchers/jugs") — served the bread of the Presence (לֶחֶם הַפָּנִים, lechem hapanim, literally "bread of the face/presence"). The final phrase אֲשֶׁר יֻסַּךְ בָּהֵן (asher yussakh bahen, "with which it shall be poured out") identifies the מְנַקִּיֹּת specifically as vessels for liquid libations — drink offerings that accompanied the bread. The LXX renders these as τρυβλία (dishes), θυΐσκας (incense cups), σπονδεῖα (libation vessels), and κύαθους (ladles). The table and its perpetual bread (replaced weekly, Lev 24:5–9) symbolized YHWH's ongoing provision and covenant fellowship — a table set in the divine presence.

### Verses 17–24 — The lampstand (menorah)
The מְנוֹרָה (menorah) is described in extraordinary detail — the most elaborate description of any single object in the tabernacle. The insistence that it was מִקְשָׁה (miqshah, "hammered work") from a single talent (כִּכָּר, kikkar) of pure gold — approximately 75 pounds (34 kg) — means the entire lampstand, with all its branches, flowers, buds, and blossoms, was beaten into shape from one massive piece of gold. No casting, no soldering, no joining. The botanical imagery is striking: the cups are described as מְשֻׁקָּדִים (meshuqqadim, "almond-shaped"), with כַּפְתֹּר (kaphtor, "bud/knob") and פֶרַח (perach, "blossom/flower"). The almond (שָׁקֵד, shaqed) is a wordplay on שֹׁקֵד (shoqed, "watching/wakeful") — the same pun God uses with Jeremiah (Jer 1:11–12): "I am watching (shoqed) over my word." The lampstand was literally a golden tree in full bloom, an image of life and light, an echo of the Tree of Life in Eden. Its seven lamps (נֵרֹת, nerot) provided the only light in the Holy Place, burning perpetually (27:20). The LXX renders λυχνίαν (lychnia, "lampstand") and preserves the same elaborate botanical description. The talent of gold used to make it was worth, by modern gold prices, well over two million dollars — a stunning concentration of beauty and value shaped entirely by hand.

### Verses 25–28 — The incense altar
The מִזְבַּח הַקְּטֹרֶת (mizbeach haqqetoret, "altar of incense") was square — one cubit by one cubit — and two cubits tall, making it taller than it was wide, a slender column of gold-overlaid wood. Its horns (קַרְנֹתָיו, qarnotav) projected from its four upper corners, formed as one piece with the altar itself. Unlike the large bronze altar of burnt offering in the courtyard, this altar was overlaid with pure gold and stood inside the Holy Place, just before the inner curtain. The incense burned on it (described in 30:34–38) filled the sacred space with fragrant smoke — a sensory embodiment of prayer ascending to God (cf. Ps 141:2; Rev 5:8; 8:3–4). The LXX renders θυσιαστήριον τοῦ θυμιάματος (altar of incense). The altar's placement and purpose created a powerful liturgical dynamic: the priest tending the incense stood between the lampstand's light and the bread of the Presence, surrounded by the fragrance of the incense rising toward the curtain that concealed the ark — an experience that engaged every sense in awareness of the holy.

### Verse 29 — The anointing oil and incense
The Hebrew רֹקֵחַ (roqeach, "perfumer/apothecary") describes the skill behind both the שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה (shemen hammishchah, "anointing oil") and the קְטֹרֶת (qetoret, "incense"). The recipes were specified in 30:22–38: the oil was a blend of myrrh, cinnamon, aromatic cane, and cassia in olive oil; the incense combined stacte, onycha, galbanum, and pure frankincense. Both were declared קֹדֶשׁ (qodesh, "holy") — set apart exclusively for sacred use, with severe penalties for unauthorized replication (30:32–33, 37–38). The LXX renders ἔργον μυρεψοῦ (work of a perfume-maker). The dynamic rendering "master perfumer" captures the specialized expertise involved — this was not ordinary mixing but a sacred craft. That both the oil (for consecrating persons and objects) and the incense (for daily worship) are mentioned together at the chapter's conclusion forms a fitting summary: the furnishings are complete, and now the means of consecrating and using them are prepared.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Vaticanus continues its significantly rearranged presentation of the tabernacle construction narrative in this section. In the MT, chapter 37 follows a clear sequence: ark (vv. 1–9), table (vv. 10–16), lampstand (vv. 17–24), incense altar (vv. 25–28), and sacred oil and incense (v. 29) — the furnishings of the interior, moving from most holy to holy. The Vaticanus text distributes this material differently: (1) Much of the content corresponding to MT 37 appears in Vaticanus within what the Greek tradition numbers as chapters 37–38, but interspersed with material the MT places in other chapters. (2) The description of the ark construction in the LXX is briefer in some respects, omitting certain repetitive details that the MT includes from the original instructions. (3) The lampstand description — one of the most elaborate passages in the MT — is preserved in the LXX but with some compression. (4) The incense altar and the sacred oil/incense appear in the Greek tradition but at different points in the construction sequence. (5) The Vaticanus text visible in the manuscript images shows the typical continuous uncial script (ΚΑΙΕΠΟΙΗΣΕΝ... "and he made...") with chapter/section markers. The overall pattern observed throughout the construction chapters continues: the LXX appears to present a more streamlined account, while the MT preserves a fuller, more deliberately repetitive narrative that closely mirrors the original divine instructions of chapters 25–30. Whether the LXX reflects an earlier, shorter Hebrew Vorlage or represents editorial condensation of a text similar to the MT remains a central question in Exodus text criticism.
